pages.title: EXPOSÉ X INTERSECTIONS ON REGULAR SURFACES

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ X
INTERSECTIONS ON REGULAR SURFACESby P. Deligne

In the first part of this exposé, after some preliminaries of intersection theory (contained in [5] ∪ [7]), we give the proof, following Artin, of a particular case of a theorem of Raynaud [9] (1.13), and an a priori proof of a theorem which Néron [8] verified case by case (1.15). From (1.13) follow fairly easily the theorems of [9] used in [2] and thereby in IX.

In the second part, I continue on my momentum and recount how the "Italians" [1], [3] computed intersection numbers on surfaces. The principal results 2.9 and 2.11 can moreover be found in Northcott [12] and [13]. To finish, I give in 2.27, 2.30, 2.34 a conjectural relation between the dimension of the versal formal scheme of moduli of a singularity of a reduced curve, the invariant \(\delta\) of the singularity and an invariant measuring its symmetry.
This exposé will not be used in the rest of the seminar.
1. Intersection numbers on arithmetic surfaces

1.0. In this § we suppose given a regular scheme \(X\) of dimension 2, a reduced divisor \(Y\) of \(X\), and a subfield \(k\) of \(H^0(Y,\mathcal O_Y)\). We suppose that \(Y\) is a curve (EGA II 7.4.1) proper over \(k\). Here are three examples of this situation.

Example 1.1. Let \((S,\eta,s)\) be a trait and \(X\) a \(S\)-scheme \(f:X\longrightarrow S\), regular, proper and flat over \(S\), with fibers purely of dimension one. We take \(Y=(f^{-1}(s))_{\mathrm{red}}\) and \(k=k(s)\). We will restrict to this case from 1.10 on.

Example 1.2. Let \(X_0\) be the spectrum of a normal local ring of dimension 2, \(s\) the closed point of \(X_0\) and \(f:X\longrightarrow X_0\) a regular connected \(X_0\)-scheme, proper over \(X_0\), distinct from \(X_0\) but coinciding with \(X_0\) above \(X_0-\{s\}\). We take \(Y=(f^{-1}(s))_{\mathrm{red}}\) and \(k=k(s)\).
Example 1.3. We take for \(X\) a regular surface, complete over a field \(k\), and for \(Y\) a reduced curve traced on \(X\).
Let us recall the following lemma (SGA 6 IV ex. 1.9).

blocks.title: Lemma 1.4.
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.4.

Let \(Z_0\) be a closed subscheme of a noetherian scheme \(Z\). Then the inclusion of \(Z_0\) in \(Z\) induces an isomorphism from the Grothendieck group of the category of coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal O_{Z_0}\)-modules to the Grothendieck group of the category of coherent sheaves on \(Z\) with support in \(Z_0\).
This lemma allows us to define the Euler-Poincaré characteristic \(\chi(\mathcal F)\) of a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal F\) on \(X\) with support in \(Y\), in such a way that,

1. (i) If \(\mathcal F\) is a coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal O_Y\)-modules, then \[\chi(\mathcal F)=\dim_k H^0(\mathcal F)-\dim_k H^1(\mathcal F)\tag{1.4.1}\]
  2. (ii) \(\chi\) is additive with respect to short exact sequences.
If the cohomology sheaves of a complex \(K\) of \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules are coherent sheaves with support in \(Y\), we set \[\chi(K)=\sum_i(-1)^i\chi(\underline H^i(K)).\tag{1.4.2}\]
If \(\mathcal F\) is localized at a point \(y\) of \(Y\), we have \[\chi(\mathcal F)=\lg_{\mathcal O_y}(\mathcal F_y)\cdot[k(y):k].\tag{1.4.3}\]
Under the hypotheses (1.1), (1.2) or (1.3), we have \[\chi(\mathcal F)=\lg f_*\mathcal F-\lg R^1f_*\mathcal F.\tag{1.4.4}\]

Let \(D\) be a divisor \(\geq0\) concentrated on \(Y\), and \(\mathcal L\) an invertible sheaf on \(D\). There is then one and only one way to define the degree \(\deg_D(\mathcal L)\) in such a way that \[\begin{cases} \deg_D(\mathcal L'\otimes\mathcal L'')=\deg_D(\mathcal L')+\deg_D(\mathcal L''),\\ \text{for }\mathcal L\subseteq\mathcal O,\quad \deg_D(\mathcal L)=-\chi(\mathcal O/\mathcal L). \end{cases}\tag{1.4.5}\]
We then have the Riemann-Roch formula on \(D\) \[\chi(\mathcal L)=\chi(\mathcal O_D)+\deg_D(\mathcal L).\tag{1.4.6}\]
Let \(D=\sum n_iD_i\) be the decomposition of \(D\) into reduced irreducible divisors; we have \[\deg_D(\mathcal L)=\sum n_i\deg_{D_i}(\mathcal L).\tag{1.4.7}\]

blocks.title: Definition 1.5.
blocks.html:
Definition 1.5.

Let \(D\) and \(E\) be two divisors \(\geq0\) on \(X\), and suppose \(D\) concentrated on \(Y\). We define the intersection number of \(D\) and \(E\) (relative to the field \(k\) (1.0)) by: \[(D,E)=\chi(\mathcal O_D\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^{\mathbf L}}\mathcal O_E) =\chi(\mathcal O_D\otimes\mathcal O_E)- \chi(\operatorname{Tor}^1(\mathcal O_D,\mathcal O_E)).\]

Under the hypotheses made, we indeed have \(\operatorname{Tor}^2(\mathcal O_D,\mathcal O_E)=0\).

blocks.title: Proposition 1.6.
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.6. Under the hypotheses of 1.5, we have:
  1. (i) The intersection number \((D,E)\) is additive in the divisors \(D\) and \(E\); this allows one, by linearity, to define the intersection number of two not necessarily positive divisors.
  2. (ii) \((D,E)=\deg_D(\mathcal O(E))\).
  3. (iii) If \(D\) and \(E\) meet only in a finite number of points, then \((D,E)=\chi(\mathcal O_D\otimes\mathcal O_E)\geq0\), equality being possible only if \(D\) and \(E\) are disjoint.
  4. (iv) If \(D\) and \(E\) are concentrated on \(Y\), then \((D,E)=(E,D)\).
  5. \(v\) If \(N\) is the conormal sheaf to \(D\), then \[(D)^2=(D,D)=-\deg_D(N).\]
  6. (vi) If \(E\), not necessarily positive, is the divisor of a meromorphic function on \(X\), then \((D,E)=0\).

Proof. Consider the exact sequence \[0\longrightarrow\mathcal O(-E)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X \longrightarrow\mathcal O_E\longrightarrow0,\] it yields, by (1.4.5), \[\begin{aligned} (D,E)&\mathrel{\mathop=^{\mathrm{dfn}}} \chi(\mathcal O_D\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^{\mathbf L}}\mathcal O_E)\\ &=\chi(\mathcal O_D\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^{\mathbf L}}\mathcal O_X) -\chi(\mathcal O_D\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^{\mathbf L}}\mathcal O(-E))\\ &=\chi(\mathcal O_D)-\chi(\mathcal O(-E)|D) =-\deg_D(\mathcal O(-E))=\deg_D(\mathcal O(E)). \end{aligned}\]

This proves (ii), (vi) and the biadditivity by virtue of (1.4.5) and (1.4.7). Under the hypotheses of (iii), we have \(\operatorname{Tor}^1(\mathcal O_D,\mathcal O_E)=0\), whence the conclusion. Under the hypotheses \(v\), we have \[\mathcal O_D\otimes\mathcal O_D=\mathcal O_D\quad\text{and}\quad \operatorname{Tor}^1(\mathcal O_D,\mathcal O_D)\simeq N\quad\text{(SGA 6 VII 2.5)},\] whence \[(D,D)=\chi(\mathcal O_D)-\chi(N)=-\deg_D(N).\]
Finally, (iv) follows from the symmetry of the tensor product.
Note that (ii), (iv) and (vi) remain true without supposing the divisors positive.

blocks.title: Proposition 1.7.
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.7.

Let \((Y_i)_{1\leq i\leq k}\) be the irreducible components of \(Y\), \(f\) a meromorphic function on \(X\), and \[\operatorname{div}(f)=\sum_i n_iY_i+Z\] its divisor, with \(|Z|\cap Y\) finite. Then, setting \(D_i=n_iY_i\), we have, for \((a_i)\in\mathbb Q^k\): \[\left(\sum_i a_iD_i\right)^2 =-\sum_{i<j}(a_i-a_j)^2(D_i,D_j)-\sum_i a_i^2(D_i,Z).\]

Proof (following [7]). By virtue of 1.6 \(v\) (vi), we have \[\begin{aligned} \left(\sum_i a_iD_i\right)^2 &=\sum_i a_i\left(D_i,\sum_j a_jD_j\right) =\sum_i a_i\left(D_i,\sum_j a_jD_j-a_i\operatorname{div}f\right)\\ &=\sum_i a_i\left(D_i,\sum_{j\ne i}(a_j-a_i)D_j-a_iZ\right)\\ &=\sum_{i\ne j}a_i(a_j-a_i)(D_i,D_j)-\sum_i a_i^2(D_i,Z)\\ &=-\sum_{i<j}(a_i-a_j)^2(D_i,D_j)-\sum_i a_i^2(D_i,Z). \end{aligned}\]

blocks.title: Corollary 1.8.
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.8.

Under the hypotheses of Example 1.1, let us take for \(f\) a uniformizer \(\pi\) of \(S\). Here, \(Z=0\) and \[\left(\sum_i k_iY_i\right)^2 =-\sum_{i<j}\frac1{n_in_j}(k_in_j-k_jn_i)^2(Y_i,Y_j)\ ;\] so the intersection form is negative, and if \(Y\) is connected, its kernel is reduced to the multiples of \(\operatorname{div}(\pi)\).

blocks.title: Corollary 1.9.
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.9. (Mumford [7]).

Under the hypotheses of Example 1.2, let us take for \(f\) a nonzero element of the maximal ideal of the local ring \(\mathcal O_s\). Here, \(Z>0\), so that the intersection form is negative definite.
1.10. We restrict ourselves, in what follows, to the case considered in Example (1.1).

Lichtenbaum [5] proved that, in this case, \(X\) is automatically projective over \(S\), so that it is not tiring to define the complex \(Rf^!\mathcal O_S\) ([4] III 8.7 p. 190). Since \(f\) is of relative dimension 1 and \(X\) and \(S\) are regular (Gorenstein would suffice) there exists ([4] V 8.3 and V 9.1) an invertible sheaf \(\omega_{X/S}\) on \(S\), unique up to unique isomorphism, such that
\[(1.10.1)\qquad Rf^!\mathcal O_S\mathrel{\sim}\omega_{X/S}[1].\]
We denote by \(K\) any divisor (effective or not) such that \(\omega\mathrel{\sim}\mathcal O(K)\).
Let \(D\) be a divisor \(\geq0\) concentrated on \(Y\) and \(N\) its conormal sheaf:
\[ \begin{array}{ccc} D&\xrightarrow{\ i\ }&X\\[-2pt] &\searrow\scriptstyle g&\downarrow\scriptstyle f\\[-2pt] &&S \end{array} \]
We have
\[(1.10.2)\qquad Rg^!\mathcal O_S=Ri^!Rf^!\mathcal O_S =Ri^!\omega_{X/S}[1]=\omega_{X/S}|D\otimes N^\vee[0].\]
By virtue of the duality theorem ([4] V 11.1 p. 210),
\[(1.10.3)\qquad Rg_*Rg^!\mathcal O_S\mathrel{\sim} R\operatorname{Hom}(Rg_*\mathcal O_D,\mathcal O_S).\]
Moreover, for every \(\mathcal O_S\)-module \(M\) concentrated at the closed point, we have
\[(1.10.4)\qquad \chi(R\operatorname{Hom}(M,\mathcal O_S))=-\chi(M)\]
so that here, using (1.4.4) and (1.10.3), we obtain
\[\chi(Rg^!\mathcal O_S)=-\chi(\mathcal O_D).\]
\[\chi(Rg^!\mathcal O_S)=\chi(\mathcal O_D)+\deg_D(Rg^!\mathcal O_S),\]
we conclude by (1.10.2) that
\[-2\chi(\mathcal O_D)=\deg_D(Rg^!\mathcal O_S) =\deg_D(\omega|D\otimes N^\vee)=\deg_D(\omega)-\deg_D(N),\]
which, by 1.6 (ii) and \(v\) can be rewritten

blocks.title: Proposition 1.11.
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.11. Under the hypotheses (1.1), for any divisor \(D\geq0\) concentrated on \(Y\), we have
\[\chi(\mathcal O_D)=-\frac12(D,D+K).\]

1.12. We will denote by \(d\) the gcd of the geometric multiplicities of the irreducible components of \(X_s=f^{-1}(s)\), i.e. the gcd of the lengths of the local rings of the maximal points of the geometric special fiber of \(f\). If \(X_\eta\) has a rational point, i.e. if \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) has a section \(x\), then \(d=1\); more precisely \(f\) is smooth at \(x(s)\). Indeed, \(x\) is a closed immersion of regular schemes, hence is a regular immersion, and we apply EGA IV 17.12.1.
Here is the proof, following M. Artin, of a particular case of a theorem of M. Raynaud [9], which only assumes \(d\) prime to the characteristic \(p\) of \(k\).

blocks.title: Theorem 1.13.
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.13. With the preceding notation, if \(Y\) is geometrically connected and if \(d=1\), then \(X\) is cohomologically flat over \(S\) in dimension 0 (EGA III 7.8.1), more precisely (EGA III 7.6.9 (ii)) we have
\[k\mathrel{\sim}\longrightarrow H^0(X_s,\mathcal O_{X_s}).\]

We easily reduce to the case where \(S\) is strictly local. As \(Y\) is geometrically connected, \(H^0(Y,\mathcal O_Y)\) is a radicial extension of \(k\), and since \((d,p)=1\), at least one of the irreducible components of \(Y\) is geometrically reduced, and one easily deduces that \(H^0(Y,\mathcal O_Y)=k\).
Let \(D\) be a divisor on \(X\) such that
\[Y\leq D<X_s.\]
Since \(d=1\), \(D\) is not a rational multiple of \(X_s\), hence by 1.8
The divisor \(Y\) therefore has an irreducible component \(E\) such that
\[0<E\leq X_s-D\quad\text{and}\quad(D,E)>0.\]
Consider the diagram

\[ \begin{array}{ccccccccc} 0&\longrightarrow&\mathcal O(-D-E)&\longrightarrow&\mathcal O&\longrightarrow&\mathcal O_{D+E}&\longrightarrow&0\\ &&\downarrow\scriptstyle\varphi&&\Vert&&\downarrow\scriptstyle\psi\\ 0&\longrightarrow&\mathcal O(-D)&\longrightarrow&\mathcal O&\longrightarrow&\mathcal O_D&\longrightarrow&0, \end{array} \]

the snake diagram provides an isomorphism between \(\operatorname{Ker}(\psi)\) and \(\operatorname{Coker}(\varphi)\). This cokernel is none other than the restriction of \(\mathcal O(-D)\) to \(E\), whence an exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow\mathcal O(-D)|E\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{D+E} \longrightarrow\mathcal O_D\longrightarrow0.\]
By construction \(\deg_E(\mathcal O(-D))=-(D,E)<0\), hence \(H^0(\mathcal O(-D)|E)=0\), and the restriction arrow
\[0\longrightarrow H^0(\mathcal O_{D+E})\longrightarrow H^0(\mathcal O_D)\]
is injective. This allows us to construct by induction a sequence of divisors
\[Y=D_0\leq D_1\leq\cdots\leq D_n=X_s\]
such that \(H^0(\mathcal O_{D_i})\) injects into \(H^0(\mathcal O_{D_{i-1}})\), hence \(H^0(\mathcal O_{X_s})=H^0(\mathcal O_Y)=k\), which completes the proof of 1.13.
The following fact is proved in [5] or [10].

blocks.title: Theorem 1.14.
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.14. (Castelnuovo, Enriques,....). Under the hypotheses (1.1), let \(D\) be a reduced irreducible divisor contained in \(Y\). Suppose that \(H^1(D,\mathcal O_D)=0\) and that, setting \(k'=H^0(D,\mathcal O_D)\), we have
\[(D,D)=-[k':k].\]

Then \(D\) is a projective line over \(k'\) and there exist a regular \(S\)-scheme \(X_1\), a closed point \(x_1\) of \(X_1\) with residue field \(k'\) and an isomorphism between \(X\) and the \(S\)-scheme deduced from \(X_1\) by blowing up \(x_1\), this isomorphism identifying \(D\) with the fiber of \(x_1\) in \(X_1\).
A divisor \(D\) possessing these properties is called an exceptional curve of the first kind.
I cannot resist the pleasure of proving the following proposition of Néron [8] prop 1' p. 90:

blocks.title: Proposition 1.15.
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.15. Suppose that \(X_\eta\) is an elliptic curve (in particular has a section) and that \(X\) contains no exceptional curve of the first kind. Then, with the notation (1.10.1), we have
\[f^*f_*\omega_{X/S}\mathrel{\sim}\longrightarrow\omega_{X/S}\]

universally.

Since \(d=1\) (1.12), it suffices, by (1.13), to prove that \(\omega_{X/S}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathcal O_X\); since \(\omega_{X/S}\) is trivial on the elliptic curve \(X_\eta\), we know a priori that
\[\omega_{X/S}\mathrel{\sim}\mathcal O\left(\sum n_iY_i\right),\]

where the \(Y_i\) are the irreducible components of \(Y\), and since \(\operatorname{div}(\pi)\sim0\), we may suppose the \(n_i\) positive. If \(\sum n_iY_i\) is proportional to the divisor \(\operatorname{div}(\pi)\), it is an integral multiple of \(\operatorname{div}(\pi)\) and we are done. Otherwise, we have
\[\left(\sum n_iY_i\right)^2<0,\]
there exists \(i\) such that
\[\left(Y_i,\sum n_jY_j\right)<0,\]
and we apply the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma 1.16.
blocks.html: Lemma 1.16. Under the hypotheses 1.1, suppose that \(Y\) is connected and that \(Y\) is reducible, or that \(\chi(\mathcal O_Y)\leq0\). Then, for an irreducible component \(Y_i\) of \(Y\) to be an exceptional curve of the first kind, it is necessary and sufficient that \((Y_i,K)<0\).
Let \(k'=H^0(Y_i,\mathcal O_{Y_i})\). The Riemann-Roch formula 1.11 gives
\[\chi(\mathcal O_{Y_i})=[k':k]\left(1-\dim_{k'}H^1(Y_i,\mathcal O_{Y_i})\right) =-\frac12\left((Y_i,Y_i)+(Y_i,K)\right).\]

If \(Y_i\) is exceptional of the first kind, we thus have \((Y_i,K)=-[k':k]<0\). Conversely, if \((Y_i,K)<0\), then \(\chi(\mathcal O_{Y_i})>0\), which implies that \(\chi(\mathcal O_{Y_i})=[k':k]\) and that \(Y\) is reducible, so that by 1.8, \((Y_i,Y_i)<0\). The negative numbers \((Y_i,Y_i)\) and \((Y_i,K)\) are divisible by \([k':k]\), so that we must have

Exercise \(a\). Under the hypotheses (1.1), we suppose that \(X_\eta\) is a principal homogeneous space under an elliptic curve and that \(X\) contains no exceptional curve of the first kind. Show that on \(X\), the Riemann-Roch formula (1.11) reads
\[\chi(\mathcal O_D)=-\frac12(D,D)\]

Exercise \(b\). Suppose in addition that the residue field \(k(s)\) is algebraically closed. Show that the diagram of the irreducible components of \(X_s\) is a multiple of one of the diagrams considered by Néron [8] p. 124–125, 4th column (cases a to c 8). This diagram determines the genera of the reduced irreducible components, their multiplicity in \(X_s\), and the nature and disposition of the singularities of \((X_s)_{\mathrm{red}}\).

We will first treat the case where \(X_s\) is irreducible; this case excluded, we will prove that every reduced irreducible component is a projective line of self-intersection \(-2\). If \(X_s=\sum n_iD_i\) is the decomposition of \(X_s\) into reduced irreducible divisors, we will rewrite this condition
\[(*)\qquad n_i=\frac12\sum_{j\ne i}n_j(D_i,D_j).\]
On the other hand, \(X_s\) is connected, so that:
\((**)\) For any \(i\) and \(j\), there exists a chain \(i=i_1,i_2,\ldots,i_n=j\) with \((D_{i_k},D_{i_{k+1}})\ne0\).
We will then seek all the solutions in integers of \((*)+(**)\). We will first show that, except in the cases b2 and c2 of Néron, the intersection numbers \((D_i,D_j)\) are equal to zero or one.
2. Italian intersections
Let \(S'\) and \(S\) be two smooth projective surfaces over a field \(k\) and \(f:S'\longrightarrow S\) a birational morphism. If \(D\) and \(E\) are two divisors on \(S\), we have
as one sees, for example, by moving \(D\) and \(E\).

It is therefore imperative to give a "birational" expression for the intersection number. We propose to explain how the Italians proceeded to do this. My principal source of information is [1], where a long bibliography can be found. See also Manin [6], and Northcott [12], [13].

(2.0) Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a regular local ring of dimension 2, \(s\) the closed point of \(S\) and \(k\) a subfield of \(k(s)\) of which \(k(s)\) is a finite extension. Intersection numbers and Euler-Poincaré characteristics will be computed in terms of \(k\).

In this §, an \(S\)-scheme \(p:S'\longrightarrow S\) will be said to be a birational transform of \(S\) if it is regular, connected, and if \(p\) is proper and induces an isomorphism from \(S'\setminus p^{-1}(s)\) to \(S\setminus\{s\}\). Everything that will be said about \(S\) will apply again to the localizations of its birational transforms at a closed point.
(2.1) Let \(f:(S'',p'')\longrightarrow(S',p')\) be a morphism of \(S\)-schemes between birational transforms of \(S\). It is then known [5] that there exists one and only one factorization of \(f\):
\[f=f_n\cdots f_1,\qquad f_i:S_{i+1}\longrightarrow S_i\]

such that \(S_{i+1}\) is deduced from \(S_i\) by blowing up a finite set \(X_i\) of closed points, and that \(f_i(X_{i+1})\subset X_i\). The set \(X_i\) is the set of points of \(S_i\) at which the projection of \(S''\) onto \(S_i\) is not an isomorphism.

In particular, \(f\) induces an isomorphism above the complement of a finite subset of \(S'\), which contains the generic points of the irreducible components of \(p'^{-1}(s)\). Consequently, \(f\) defines an injection of the set \(\operatorname{Irr}(p'^{-1}(s))\) of irreducible components of \(p'^{-1}(s)\) into \(\operatorname{Irr}(p''^{-1}(s))\).

The birational transforms of \(S\) have no \(S\)-automorphism. We will denote here by \(I\) the set of \(S\)-isomorphism classes of the birational transforms of \(S\), ordered by the relation of domination. The set \(I\) is directed, and has smallest element \(S\).

blocks.title: Definition 2.2.
blocks.html:
Definition 2.2. The set \(S^*\) of points of \(S\) infinitely near \(s\) is the inductive limit set
\[S^*=\varinjlim_I\operatorname{Irr}(p^{-1}(s)).\]

If an element \(t\in S^*\) is represented on a birational transform \(S'\) of \(S\) by a reduced irreducible complete curve \(T\), the field \(H^0(T,\mathcal O_T)\) depends only on \(t\), and not on the choice of \(S'\); it is called the residue field \(k(t)\) of \(t\).

Let \(S_1\) be the blow-up of \(S\) along \(s\). The projective line \(s^*\), inverse image of \(\{s\}\) in \(S_1\), is called the curvetta of \(s\) and defines an element \(s^*\) of \(S^*\) with residue field \(k(s)\).

Let \(S'\) be a birational transform of \(S\), \(t\) a closed point of \(S'\) and \(S'_t\) the spectrum of the local ring of \(S'\) at \(t\). One defines in an obvious way an injection of \(S_t'^*\) into \(S^*\); in particular, \(t\) defines an element \(t^*\) of \(S^*\), with the same residue field. We denote by \(S'^{**}\) the union in \(S^*\) of the \(S_t'^*\) for \(t\) closed in \(S'\).
The following proposition follows from the description 2.1 of the birational transforms of \(S\):

blocks.title: Proposition 2.3.
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3. For any \(t\in S^*\), there exists one and only one sequence of birational transforms of \(S\), \((S_i)_{0\leq i\leq n+1}\), and of closed points \(t_i\in S_i\), \((0\leq i\leq n)\), such that
  1. (i) \(S_0=S\), and \(S_{i+1}\) is the blow-up of \(S_i\) along \([t_i]\);
  2. (ii) \(t_{i+1}\in t_i^*\), \((0\leq i<n)\), and \(t=t_n^*\).

Moreover, for any birational transform \((S',p')\) of \(S\), in order that \(t\) belong to the image of \(\operatorname{Irr}(p'^{-1}(s))\) in \(S^*\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(S'\) dominate \(S_{n+1}\).

We call \(n\) the order of \(t\). We will denote here by \(S(t)\) (resp. by \(S_+(t)\)) the birational transform \(S_n\) (resp. \(S_{n+1}\)) of \(S\). By abuse of notation, we will again denote by \(t\) the closed point \(t_n\) of \(S_n\) and by \(t^*\) its inverse image \(t_n^*\) in \(S_{n+1}\).
We equip \(S^*\) with the following order:
\[t_1\prec t_2\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad S_+(t_2)\text{ dominates }S_+(t_1).\]

If \(t_1\prec t_2\), then \(k(t_2)\) is an extension of \(k(t_1)\). The ordered set \(S^*\) has smallest element \(s^*\), and every bounded-above subset of \(S^*\) is finite and totally ordered (\(S^*\) is a "tree with root \(s^*\)"). Moreover, the order \(n\) of \(t\in S^*\) is the distance, in the tree \(S^*\), from \(s^*\) to \(t\) (\(n=\# [s^*,t[\)).
The following proposition makes explicit the description 2.1 of the birational transforms of \(S\).

blocks.title: Proposition 2.4.
blocks.html: Proposition 2.4. The function which, to a birational transform \(p:S'\longrightarrow S\) of \(S\), associates the subset \(\operatorname{Irr}(p^{-1}(s))\) of \(S^*\) induces a bijection between the set \(I\) (2.1) and the set of finite subsets of \(S^*\) which, with any element, contain its predecessors.

Let \(D\) be a divisor \(\geq0\) on \(S\) and \(t\in S^*\). We say that \(t\) belongs to \(D\) if the closed point \(t\) of \(S(t)\) (2.3) belongs to the strict transform (= schematic closure of \(D-\{s\}\)) of \(D\) on \(S(t)\).

blocks.title: Definition 2.5.
blocks.html: Definition 2.5. Let \(t,t'\in S^*\). We say that \(t'\) is proximate to \(t\) if \(t'\in S_+(t)^*\) (2.2) and belongs to the curvetta \(t^*\) of \(t\) (2.3).

If \(t'\) is an immediate successor of \(t\), then \(t'\) is proximate to \(t\); if in a sequence \((t_i)_{0\leq i\leq n}\) each \(t_{i+1}\) is an immediate successor of \(t_i\), and if \(t_n\) is proximate to \(t_0\), then \(t_i\) is proximate to \(t_0\) for \(0<i\leq n\) and \(t_n\) is not proximate to \(t_i\) for \(0<i\leq n-1\).

(2.6) Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring of dimension \(n\), \(T\) its spectrum, \(m\) its maximal ideal, \(k=A/m\) its residue field and \(P=\sum_{0\leq i\leq n}a_iX^i\) its Hilbert-Samuel polynomial:
\[P(r)=\dim_k(m^r/m^{r+1})\qquad\text{for }r\text{ large}.\]
Recall that the multiplicity \(\mu_s\) in \(T\) of the closed point \(s\) satisfies by definition
When \(n=1\), we thus simply have
\[\mu_s=\dim_k m^r/m^{r+1}\qquad\text{for }r\text{ large}.\]
In geometric terms, \(\mu_s\) can be defined as the degree, in the projective space over \(k\), \(\mathbb P_k(m/m^2)\), of the special fiber of the blow-up of \(T\) along the subscheme \(\{s\}\).

Recall that if \(Y\) and \(Z\) are two closed subschemes of a scheme \(X\), then the strict transform of \(Y\) in the blow-up \(\widetilde X\) of \(X\) along \(Z\), the schematic closure of \(Y\setminus Z\) in \(\widetilde X\), is none other than the blow-up of \(Y\) along \(Y\cap Z\).

Suppose that \(T\) is a closed subscheme of a regular scheme \(U\). Let \(p:U_1\longrightarrow U\) be the blow-up of \(U\) along \(\{s\}\), \(s^*\) the inverse image of \(s\) in \(U_1\) and \(T'\) the strict transform of \(T\) in \(U_1\). What precedes shows that the multiplicity of \(T\) at \(s\) is the degree in the projective space \(s^*\) of the subscheme \(s^*\cap T'\).

Suppose moreover that \(T\) is a divisor of \(U\), let \(T^*\) be its total transform in \(U_1\) (the inverse image divisor) and define \(\nu\) by the formula \(T^*=\nu s^*+T'\). If \(X\) is a line of \(s^*\), and if we compute the Euler-Poincaré characteristics as alternating sums of dimensions over \(k\), it is known that
\[(2.6.1)\qquad (X,s^*)=\chi\left(X\mathbin{\overset{\mathbb L}{\otimes}}\mathcal O_{s^*}\right) =\deg_X\mathcal O(s^*)=-1\]
and we have verified that
\[(2.6.2)\qquad (X,T')=\chi\left(X\mathbin{\overset{\mathbb L}{\otimes}}\mathcal O_{T'}\right) =\deg_{s^*}(s^*\cap T')=\mu_s.\]
The projection formula

\[Rp_*\left(\mathcal O_X\mathbin{\overset{\mathbb L}{\otimes}}\mathcal O_{T^*}\right) =Rp_*\left(\mathcal O_X\mathbin{\overset{\mathbb L}{\otimes}}Lp^*\mathcal O_T\right) =Rp_*\mathcal O_X\mathbin{\overset{\mathbb L}{\otimes}}\mathcal O_T\]
and its corollary
yields, by (2.6.1), (2.6.2), the identity

blocks.title: Proposition 2.7.
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.7. Let \(T\) be a divisor \(\geq0\) in a regular scheme \(U\), \(s\) a closed point of \(U\), \(U_1\) the blow-up of \(U\) along \(\{s\}\), \(s^*\) the inverse image of \(s\) in \(U_1\), \(T'\) the strict transform of \(T\) on \(U_1\) and \(T^*\) its total transform. There is an identity between
  1. (i) The multiplicity, in the sense of Samuel, of the point \(s\) of \(T\).
  2. (ii) The degree, in the projective space \(s^*\), of \(s^*\cap T'\).
  3. (iii) The multiplicity of \(s^*\) in the divisor \(T^*\).

2.8. Let us return to the hypotheses of 2.0. Let \(D\) be a divisor \(\geq0\) on \(S\) and \(t\in S^*\). The multiplicity \(\mu_t(D)\) of \(D\) at \(t\) is, by definition, the multiplicity at \(t\) of the strict transform of \(D\) on \(S(t)\) (2.3). The function of \(t\), \(\mu_t(D)\), has for support the set of points infinitely near \(s\) which belong to \(D\) (2.4). When \(D\) is regular, the numbers \(\mu_t(D)\cdot[k(t):k(s)]\) are equal to zero or one: indeed, the strict transforms \(D'\) of \(D\) are isomorphic to \(D\), and we apply the definition (2.6) of multiplicities.
Intersection numbers are computed in terms of multiplicities:

blocks.title: Theorem 2.9.
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.9. If the divisors \(D\) and \(E\) meet only at \(s\), we have
\[(D,E)=\sum_{t\in S^*}\mu_t(D)\mu_t(E)[k(t):k]\]

the sum being extended over the points infinitely near \(s\) common to \(D\) and \(E\).
Proof. Let \(p:S'\longrightarrow S\) be a birational transform of \(S\). We denote by \(D^*\) and \(E^*\) (resp. by \(D'\) and \(E'\)) the total (resp. strict) transforms of \(D\) and \(E\).

blocks.title: Lemma 2.9.1.
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.9.1.
  1. (i) The total transform \(D^*\) is characterized by:
    1. \(a\) \(D^*-D'\) is concentrated on \(f^{-1}(s)\)
    2. \(b\) if \(X\) is concentrated on \(f^{-1}(s)\), then \((D^*,X)=0\).
  2. (ii) \((D,E)=(D^*,E^*)=(D^*,E')=(D',E^*)\).
Assertion (i)\(a\) is trivial, and (i)\(b\) follows from 1.6 (vi).
That \(a\) and \(b\) determine \(D^*\) follows from 1.9. By construction, the cokernel of the arrow from \(\mathcal O_E\) to \(f_*\mathcal O_{E'}\) is finite, whence

\[(D^*,E')=\chi\left(f^*\mathcal O_D\mathbin{\overset{\mathbb L}{\otimes}}\mathcal O_{E'}\right) =\chi\left(\mathcal O_D\mathbin{\overset{\mathbb L}{\otimes}}f_*\mathcal O_{E'}\right) =\chi\left(\mathcal O_D\mathbin{\overset{\mathbb L}{\otimes}}\mathcal O_E\right)=(D,E)\]
which, by (i), implies (ii).
Take for \(S'\) the blow-up of \(S\) at \(s\). By (2.9.1), (2.6.3) and 2.7, we have
Theorem 2.9 is now proved by induction on \((D,E)\), by an iterated application of (2.9.2).

(2.10) Let \(A\) be a reduced noetherian local ring of dimension one, \(s\) the closed point of \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(\widetilde A\) the normalization of \(A\) and \(k\) a field of which \(k(s)\) is a finite extension. Suppose that \(\widetilde A\) is finite over \(A\) (i.e. that the completion \(\widehat A\) is reduced). We will call the invariant \(\delta\) of the singularity of \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) at \(s\) the length, computed over \(k\), of the \(A\)-module \(\widetilde A/A\).

blocks.title: Theorem 2.11.
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.11. Let \(D\) be a reduced curve traced on \(S\). Then:
  1. (i) For any \(t\in S^*\), we have, with the notation of 2.8 \[\mu_t(D)=\sum_{t'\text{ close to }t}\mu_{t'}(D)[k(t'):k(t)].\]
  2. (ii) Suppose the normalization \(\widetilde D\) of \(D\) finite over \(D\), and let \(\delta=\chi(\mathcal O_{\widetilde D}/\mathcal O_D)\) be the invariant of the singularity of \(D\) at \(s\). For all \(t\in S^*\), with the exception of a finite number, \(\mu_t(D)\) is equal to zero or one, and we have \[\delta=\sum_{t\in S^*}\frac12\mu_t(D)(\mu_t(D)-1)[k(t):k].\]
Let \(p:S_1\longrightarrow S\) be the blow-up of \(S\) along \(\{s\}\), \(D^*\) the total transform of \(D\) and \(D'\) its strict transform. By virtue of (2.7), we have
The curvetta \(s^*\) is nonsingular, so that, by 2.8 and Definition 2.5, formula 2.9, applied to the divisors \(s^*\) and \(D'\) of \(S_1\), reads
\[(2.11.3)\qquad (s^*,D')=\sum_{t'\text{ close to }s}\mu_{t'}(D)[k(t'):k].\]
Assertion (i) for \(s=t\) follows from (2.11.2) and (2.11.3), and the general case is obtained by applying this result to the localization at \(t\) of \(S(t)\) (2.3).
Denote by \(m\) the maximal ideal which defines \(\{s\}\), and let \(n\geq0\). It is known (SGA VII 3.5) that
\[(2.11.4)\qquad p_*\mathcal O_{S_1}(-ns^*)=m^n\quad\text{and}\quad R^ip_*\mathcal O_{S_1}(-ns^*)=0\quad(i>0).\]
We deduce from this, and from the exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{S_1}(-ns^*)\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{S_1} \longrightarrow\mathcal O_{ns^*}\longrightarrow0\]
that
\[(2.11.5)\qquad p_*\mathcal O_{ns^*}=\mathcal O_S/m^n\quad\text{and}\quad R^ip_*\mathcal O_{ns^*}=0\quad(i>0)\]
and that
\[(2.11.6)\qquad \chi(\mathcal O_{ns^*})=\chi(\mathcal O_S/m^n) =\frac{n(n+1)}2[k(s):k].\]
Take \(n=0\) in (2.11.4). For any complex \(K\) of coherent sheaves on \(S\), we have by the "projection formula"
\[K\mathrel{\sim}\longrightarrow Rp_*Lp^*K \quad(\simeq K\mathbin{\overset{\mathbb L}{\otimes}}Rp_*\mathcal O_{S_1}).\]
In particular, since \(Lf^*\mathcal O_D=f^*\mathcal O_D=\mathcal O_{D^*}\), we have
\[(2.11.7)\qquad p_*\mathcal O_{D^*}=\mathcal O_D\quad\text{and}\quad R^ip_*\mathcal O_{D^*}=0\quad(i>0).\]
The exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{D^*}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{D'}\oplus\mathcal O_E \longrightarrow\mathcal O_{D'\cap E}\longrightarrow0\]
yields by direct image, thanks to (2.11.7), exact sequences
\[0\longrightarrow\mathcal O_D\longrightarrow f_*\mathcal O_{D'}\oplus f_*\mathcal O_E \longrightarrow f_*\mathcal O_{D'\cap E}\longrightarrow0\]
and
\[0\longrightarrow f_*\mathcal O_D/\mathcal O_D\longrightarrow f_*\mathcal O_E \longrightarrow f_*\mathcal O_{D'\cap E}\longrightarrow0.\]
By (2.11.6) and (2.11.8), we therefore have
\[(2.11.9)\qquad \chi(f_*\mathcal O_{D'}/\mathcal O_D) =\frac12\mu_s(D)(\mu_s(D)+1)[k(s):k]-\mu_s(D)^2[k(s):k]\] \[=\frac12\mu_s(D)(\mu_s(D)-1)[k(s):k].\]

The schemes \(D\) and \(D'\) are reduced, differ only at \(s\), and \(D'\) is finite over \(D\). These schemes therefore have the same normalization, and the invariant \(\delta\) of the singularity of \(D\) at \(s\) is the sum of \(\chi(f_*\mathcal O_{D'}/\mathcal O_D)\) and of the sum of the invariants \(\delta\) of the singularities of \(D'\) at the points of \(D'\cap s^*\). Let us prove 2.11 (ii) by induction on \(\delta\). If \(\mu_s(D)>1\), the induction hypothesis applies to \(D'\), and 2.11 (ii) follows from (2.11.9). If \(\mu_s(D)=1\), then \((D',s^*)=1\) and \(D'\cap s^*\) is a regular divisor of \(D'\); by (EGA IV 19.1.1), \(D'\) is regular; by (2.11.9), \(D=D'\) is itself regular and 2.11 (ii) follows from 2.8.

2.12. Let \(t\in S^*\) and let \(S'\) be a birational transform of \(S\) dominating \(S_+(t)\) (2.3). We will call total transform of \(t\) over \(S'\), and again denote by \(t^*\), the total transform on \(S'\) of the divisor \(t^*\) of \(S_+(t)\).
Let \(\nu\) be a function with finite support from \(S^*\) to \(\mathbb N\), and let us suppose that \(\nu\) satisfies, for every \(t\in S^*\)
\[(2.12.1)\qquad \nu(t)\geq \sum_{t'\text{ close to }t}\nu(t')[k(t'):k(t)]\]

(inequality of proximate points). Let \(f:S'\longrightarrow S\) be a birational transform of \(S\) dominating the \(S_+(t)\) for \(t\) in the support of \( u\), and \( u^*\) the divisor \[\sum_{t\in S^*} u(t)t^*\quad ext{on }S'.\]
We will denote by \(I(\nu)\) the ideal of \(\mathcal O_S\) which is the direct image under \(f\) of the ideal of \(\mathcal O_{S'}\) defining the divisor \(\nu^*\):
\[I(\nu)=f_*\mathcal O(-\nu^*).\]
By (2.11.4), this ideal does not depend on the choice of \(S'\). The ideals of \(S\) obtained by this procedure are those that Zariski [11] calls "complete".

blocks.title: Theorem 2.13.
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.13. Under the preceding hypotheses, we have
\[\chi(\mathcal O_S/I(\nu))=\sum_{t\in S^*}\frac{\nu(t)(\nu(t)+1)}2[k(t):k]\]

and

\[R^if_*\mathcal O_{S'}(-\nu^*)=0\quad\text{for }i>0.\]
In old French, this is expressed, briefly, by saying that:

"requiring that a plane curve pass \(n\) times through a point \(t\), projective or infinitely near, imposes \(\frac{n(n+1)}2\) conditions, in general independent, on the coefficients of its equation".
Let us first prove the lemma

blocks.title: Lemma 2.14.
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.14. Let \(p:S_1\longrightarrow S\) be the blow-up of \(S\) along \(\{s\}\), \(T\) a closed subscheme of \(S_1\), with ideal \(q\), \(D\) a divisor, \(n\) an integer, \(J=\mathcal O(-s^*)\) the ideal of \(S_1\) defining the divisor \(s^*\), \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{S_1}/J^nq)\), \(D\) a regular divisor on \(S\) and \(D'\) its strict transform on \(S_1\). We suppose \(T\cap s^*\) and \(T\cap D'\) finite.
  1. (i) If \(\lg_{k(s)}(T\cap s^*)\leq n+1\), we have \[R^ip_*(qJ^n)=0\quad\text{for }i>0\quad\text{and, for }T\text{ artinian},\] \[\lg_{k(s)}(\mathcal O_S/p_*(qJ^n))=\frac{n(n+1)}2+\lg_{k(s)}(T).\]
  2. (ii) If \(\lg_{k(s)}(T\cap s^*)\leq n\), we have \[\lg_{k(s)}\bigl(D\cap\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_S/p_*(qJ^n))\bigr) =n+\lg_{k(s)}(D'\cap T).\]
Consider the exact sequence on \(S_1\)
\[(2.14.1)\qquad0\longrightarrow J^nq\longrightarrow J^n \xrightarrow{u}J^n\otimes\mathcal O_T\longrightarrow0.\]
To prove that \(R^ip_*J^nq=0\) \((i>0)\), it suffices, by (2.11.4), to show that the arrow \(p_*(u)\) is surjective. By the theorem on holomorphic functions, it suffices to prove that the arrows
\[u_k:p_*(J^n\otimes\mathcal O/J^k)\longrightarrow p_*(J^n\otimes\mathcal O_T\otimes\mathcal O/J^k)\]
are surjective.

We will denote by \(\operatorname{Gr}_J\) an associated graded for the \(J\)-adic filtration. The module \(J^m/J^{m+1}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathcal O_{s^*}(m)\); we thus have \(R^1p_*J^m/J^{m+1}=0\) and the arrow from \(p_*(J^n/J^{n+k+1})\) to \(p_*(J^n/J^{n+k})\) is surjective. To prove, by induction on \(k\), that the arrows \(u_k\) are surjective, it thus suffices to prove the surjectivity of the \(v_k\):
\[v_k:p_*(J^n\otimes\operatorname{Gr}_J^k(\mathcal O))\longrightarrow p_*(J^n\otimes\operatorname{Gr}_J^k(\mathcal O_T))\qquad(k\geq0).\]

The module \(\operatorname{Gr}_J^k(\mathcal O_T)\) is a quotient of \(J^k/J^{k+1}\otimes\mathcal O_T\); it is therefore of length over \(k(s)\) at most \(n+1\). The kernel \(N\) of the surjective arrow
\[v'_k:J^n\otimes\operatorname{Gr}_J^k(\mathcal O)\simeq\mathcal O_{s^*}(n+k) \longrightarrow J^n\otimes\operatorname{Gr}_J^k(\mathcal O_T)\]
is therefore an invertible sheaf on the projective line \(s^*\), of degree \(\geq-1\). We have \(R^1p_*N=0\) and \(v_k=p_*(v'_k)\) is surjective.
If \(T\) is artinian, from the exact sequence (2.14.1) we now deduce that
\[\lg_{k(s)}(p_*J^n/p_*(J^nq))=\lg_{k(s)}(\mathcal O_T),\]
and 2.14 (i) follows from (2.11.4) and (2.11.6).
Let \(I_{D'\cap T}\) be the (principal) ideal of \(\mathcal O_D\) defining \(D'\cap T\). Under the hypotheses of (ii), the exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow I_{D'\cap T}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{D'} \longrightarrow\mathcal O_{D'\cap T}\longrightarrow0\]
shows that \(D'\cap T\) satisfies hypothesis (i).

The projection \(p\) establishes an isomorphism between \(D\) and \(D'\); under this isomorphism, to the ideal of \(\mathcal O_D\) defining the subscheme \(D\cap\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_S/p_*(qJ^n))\) of \(D\) corresponds the ideal of \(D'\) generated by the global sections of \(qJ^n\) on \(S_1\). This ideal is contained in the ideal of \(D'\) defining \(D'\cap Y\). It is in fact equal to it, for every section of \(\mathcal O_{D'\cup Y}\) extends to a section of \(\mathcal O_{S_1}\): if \(I_{D'\cup Y}\) is the ideal defining \(D'\cup Y\), the obstruction to extending lies in \(R^1p_*(I_{D'\cup Y})\). We have
\[I_{D'\cup Y}=\mathcal O(-D')\cap qJ^n=(\mathcal O(-D')\cap J^n)\cap qJ^n =\mathcal O(-D')J^n\cap qJ^n\] \[=(\mathcal O(-D')\cap q)\cdot J^n\]
and, by (i) applied to \(D'\cup T\), there is no obstruction.
We thus have
\[(2.14.2)\qquad\lg_{k(s)}\bigl(D\cap\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_S/p_*(qJ^n))\bigr) =\lg_{k(s)}(D'\cap Y).\]
From the isomorphism
\[J^n\otimes\mathcal O_{D'}/q\mathcal O_{D'} \xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal O_{D'}J^n/\mathcal O_{D'}qJ^n\]
we deduce that the following sequence is exact
\[0\longrightarrow J^n\otimes\mathcal O_{D'\cap T} \longrightarrow\mathcal O_{D'\cap Y}\longrightarrow\mathcal O_{D'\cap n s^*}\longrightarrow0,\]
so that
Let us now prove, by induction on the number of elements in the support of \(\nu\), the assertions of 2.13 and the following assertion:
\[(2.14.4)\qquad\text{If }D\text{ is a regular divisor on }S,\text{ then,}\] \[\chi(D\cap\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_S/I(\nu))) =\sum_{t\text{ on }D}\nu(t)[k(t):k].\]

If \( u=0\), then \( u^*=0\), \(I( u)=\mathcal O_S\) and 2.13 and (2.14.4) are trivial (the second assertion of (2.13) follows from an iterated application of (2.11.4) for \(n=0\)). Otherwise, we have \( u(s)>0\), and there exists an \(S\)-morphism \(g:S'\longrightarrow S_1\). For each closed point \(a\) of \(S_1\), let \(S_{1a}\) be the localization of \(S_1\) at \(a\), \( u_a\) the restriction of \( u\) to \(S_{1a}^*\), \(S'_a\) the localization \(S'=S' imes_{S_1}S_{1a}\), \[I( u_a)=g_*\mathcal O_{S'}(- u_a^*),\qquad T_a=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{S_1}/I( u_a)),\qquad T= igcup T_a\quad ext{(finite disjoint union)}\]
and \(q\) the ideal of \(T\). We have, with the notations of 2.14
The induction hypothesis applies to the \((S_{1a},S'_a,\nu_a)\). We thus have
\[(2.14.6)\qquad R^ig_*\mathcal O_{S'}(-\nu^*)=0\quad\text{for }i>0\] \[(2.14.7)\qquad \chi(T)= \sum_{t\in S^*-\{s\}}\frac{\nu(t)(\nu(t)+1)}2[k(t):k]\]
and, by (2.14.4) applied to the divisor \(s^*\) of \(S_1\) and the hypothesis (2.12.1)
\[\lg_{k(s)}(T\cap s^*)= \sum_{t\text{ close to }s}\nu(t)[k(t):k(s)]\leq\nu(s).\]
This inequality allows us to apply 2.14; from (2.14.5), we get
\[R^ip_*g_*\mathcal O(-\nu^*)=0\quad\text{for }i>0\] \[\lg_{k(s)}(\mathcal O_S/p_*g_*\mathcal O(-\nu^*))= \frac{\nu(s)(\nu(s)+1)}2+\lg_{k(s)}(T)\]
so that the assertions (2.13) follow from (2.14.6) and (2.14.7).
Finally, 2.14 (ii) yields
\[\lg_{k(s)}(D\cap\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_S/I(\nu)))= \nu(s)+\lg_{k(s)}(D'\cap T),\]
that is, by (2.14.4) applied to \(D'\),
\[\chi(D\cap\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_S/I(\nu)))= \nu(s)[k(s):k]+\sum_{t\text{ on }D'}\nu(t)[k(t):k] =\sum_{t\text{ on }D}\nu(t)[k(t):k].\]
This completes the proof of 2.13.
(2.15) Fragments of what precedes generalize to higher dimension.

Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a regular local ring of dimension \(n\) with closed point \(s\), \(k(s)\) being a finite extension of \(k\). We may again consider the set \(I\) of schemes \(f:S'\longrightarrow S\) deduced from \(S\) by successive blow-ups of closed points, and set as in 2.1
\[S^*=\varinjlim_{S'\in I}\operatorname{Irr}(f^{-1}(s)).\]
\(S^*\) will again be called the set of points of \(S\) infinitely near to \(s\).
If \(X\) is a closed subscheme of \(S\), and if \(t\in S^*\), we define as in 2.8 the multiplicity of \(X\) at \(t\).

Let \(f:S'\longrightarrow S\) be a composite of blow-up morphisms of closed points, \(D\) a divisor on \(S\), \(D^*=f^*D\) its total transform on \(S'\), \(E\) a curve on \(S\), \(E'\) its strict transform on \(S'\), and suppose that \(D\cap E\) is reduced to \(s\). The projection formula again gives
Proceeding as in 2.9, we prove with the help of (2.16) that
Let us define the proximate points as in 2.5. Proceeding as in 2.11, we check that
\[(2.18)\qquad \mu_t(E)=\sum_{t'\text{ close to }t}\mu_{t'}(E)[k(t'):k].\]
On the other hand, formula 2.11 (ii) becomes false in general (see the examples 2.25).
2.19. Let \(S\) be a projective regular surface over a field \(k\). We denote by \(S^*\) the disjoint sum, extended over the closed points of \(S\)
\[S^*=\coprod_{s\in S}\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{S,s})^*\]
and we call \(S^*\) the set of points infinitely near to the points of \(S\).

Let \(I\) be the set of surfaces deduced from \(S\) by successive blow-ups of closed points. The set \(I\) is cofinal in the set of regular models of the function field of \(S\), ordered by domination. For \(i\in I\), we denote by \(S_i\) the corresponding surface, equipped with \(p_i:S_i\longrightarrow S\). We will denote by \(\operatorname{Div}^{\pm}(S_i)\) the group of divisors, positive or not, on \(S_i\).

Definition 2.20.

(i) The group of "curves" on \(S\) is the inductive limit set

\[\varinjlim_{i\in I}\operatorname{Div}^{\pm}(S_i).\]

(ii) The category of "invertible sheaves" on \(S\) is the inductive limit (SGA 4 VI 6) of the categories of invertible sheaves on the \((S_i)_{i\in I}\).
These objects are birational invariants of \(S\).
Example 2.21. (i) Each "curve" \(C\) on \(S\) defines an "invertible sheaf" \(\mathcal O(C)\) on \(S\).
(ii) Each divisor \(C\) on \(S\) defines a "curve" on \(S\), which will be denoted by \(C^*\).
(iii) If \(t\in S^*\), we define as in 2.3 the surface \(S_+(t)\) and the divisor \(t^*\) on \(S_+(t)\); we again denote by \(t^*\) the "curve" defined by \(t^*\).
(2.22) One easily checks that a "curve" \(C\) (resp. an "invertible sheaf" \(\mathcal L\)) can be written in one and only one way in the form
\[C=C_0^*-\sum_{t\in S^*}i_t\,t^* \qquad\text{resp.}\qquad \mathcal L=\mathcal L_0\otimes\mathcal O\!\left(-\sum_{t\in S^*}i_t\,t^*\right)\]

where \(C_0\) (resp. \(\mathcal L_0\)) is a divisor (resp. an invertible sheaf) on \(S\) and where the \(i_t\) are integers, almost all zero. We say that \(C\) (resp. \(\mathcal L\)) is the curve \(C_0\) (resp. the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal L_0\)) endowed with virtual multiplicities \(i_t\).

The completion \(\widehat{\operatorname{Div}}{}^{\pm}(S)\times\mathbb Z^{S^*}\) of the group \(\operatorname{Div}^{\pm}(S)\times\mathbb Z^{(S^*)}\) of "curves" on \(S\) is again a birational invariant of \(S\); it is called the group of "generalized curves" on \(S\). On \(S\), a "generalized curve" \(C\) is repre- sented by a divisor \(C_0\), endowed with a possibly infinite number of virtual multiplicities.
One defines similarly the "generalized invertible sheaves" on \(S\).

Example 2.23. Suppose \(k\) perfect. Then the invertible sheaf \(\Omega^2_{S/k}\), endowed with a virtual multiplicity (-1) at each point \(t\in S^*\), is a birational invariant of \(S\), say \(\Omega^{2*}_{S/k}\).

Example 2.24. Let \(f:S_1\longrightarrow S_2\) be a dominant morphism of regular pro- jective surfaces over \(k\). One defines without difficulty the inverse image of a "curve" or of an "invertible sheaf" on \(S_2\), by passing to a model of \(S_2\) where this "curve" or "invertible sheaf" is an ordinary divisor or invertible sheaf. By passage to the limit (I have not checked whether this passage to the limit is possible), one defines similarly the inverse image of a "generalized curve", or of a "generalized invertible sheaf". Suppose \(k\) perfect and \(f\) generically étale. The different \(\mathfrak D(f)\) of \(f\) is then the positive divisor on \(S_1\) satisfying
\[f^*\Omega^2_{S_2/k}(\mathfrak D(f))\mathrel{\sim}\Omega^2_{S_1/k}.\]
This different is not a birational invariant of \(f\); to obtain a birational invariant, one must consider the "generalized curve" of codifferents
\[\mathfrak D^*(f)=\mathfrak D(f)+\sum_{t\in S_1^*}t^*-f^*\!\left(\sum_{t\in S_2^*}t^*\right)\]
or, briefly
This "generalized curve" has support contained in the closed subset of \(S_2\) where \(f\) is not étale.
(2.25) Example.

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field and \(\mathbb E_n\) the affine space of \(n\) dimensions over \(k\). Let \(C\) be a reduced curve in \(\mathbb E_n\), having a sin- gularity at the origin. To this singularity we associate its "Enriques diagram" (cf. [3]), which is the graph whose vertices are the points infinitely near to (0) on \(C\) (2.4 and 2.15), each vertex being joined by an edge to those proximate to it (2.5 and 2.19). This diagram determines the multiplicities of these points on \(C\) (2.11 and 2.18) and, for \(n=2\), the invariant \(\delta\) (2.10 and 2.11) of the singularity of \(C\) at the origin.
For the simplest plane singularities, one finds:

　╲1　1　1　1……

If \(n>2\), the invariant \(\delta\) is no longer determined by this diagram. Thus, the union of 3 concurrent coplanar (resp. non-coplanar) lines has invariant \(\delta=3\) (resp. \(\delta=2\)), while the Enriques diagram is the same. I do not know whether this phenomenon also occurs for unibranch curves.

2.26. Theorem 2.27 below was stated in the form of a false conjec- ture in the first version (distributed by the IHES) of this exposé. It owes much to conversations with D. S. Rim, some of whose results it encompasses (D. S. Rim, torsion differentials and deformations, theorem 2.7 (i)).
Let \(C\) be a reduced projective curve over an algebraically closed field \(k\), and \(s\in C(k)\). We will denote by

\(A_s\): the local ring of \(C\) at \(s\) (or its completion, which would come to the same thing for what follows);
\(\widetilde A_s\): the normalization of \(A_s\);
\(\delta(s):\dim_k(\widetilde A_s/A_s)\);
\(m_0(s)\): the dimension of the formal versal scheme \(T\) of deformations of \(\operatorname{Spec}(A_s)\).
For \(E\) an irreducible component of \(T\), we will again set \[m_0^E(s)=\dim(E).\]

The modules \(\tau^{\sim}\) of derivations of \(\widetilde A_s\) and \(\tau\) of derivations of \(A_s\) are torsion-free; they inject into the module of derivations of the field of fractions of \(A_s\), for \(A_s\) integral, and into the module of derivations of the total ring of fractions of \(A_s\) (a product of fields) in the general case. Moreover, via these inclusions, \(\tau\) and \(\tau^{\sim}\) are commensurable: \(\tau/\tau\cap\tau^{\sim}\) and \(\tau^{\sim}/\tau\cap\tau^{\sim}\) are of finite dimension over \(k\). We set
\[m_1(s)=[\tau^{\sim}:\tau]=_{\mathrm{dfn}} \dim_k(\tau^{\sim}/\tau\cap\tau^{\sim})-\dim_k(\tau/\tau\cap\tau^{\sim}).\]

blocks.title: Theorem 2.27.
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.27. Let \(E\) be an irreducible component of the formal versal scheme \(T\) of deformations of \(A_s\). If "the deformation of \(A_s\) over the generic point \(e\) of \(E\) is smooth", i.e. if the image of the formal subscheme of non-smoothness (defined by a jacobian ideal: VI 5), finite over \(T\), does not contain \(e\), then
\[3\,\delta(s)=m_0^E(s)+m_1(s).\]
The validity of 2.27 depends only on the completion of \(A_s\). Replacing \((C,s)\) by \((C',s)\) formally isomorphic to \((C,s)\) at \(s\), we may suppose that the following conditions are satisfied

1. \(a\) \(s\) is the only singular point of \(C\);
  2. \(b\) \(C\) has no infinitesimal automorphisms.

Condition \(b\) guarantees that the deformation functor of \(C\) is prorepresentable. It is even effectively pro-representable, for there is no obstruction to lifting a given (ample) invertible sheaf on \(C\). Let \(f:X_0\longrightarrow T_0\) be the universal deformation of \(C\) and \(t_0\) the closed point of \(T_0\): \(C\simeq X_0{}_{t_0}\).

blocks.title: Lemma 2.28.
blocks.html: Lemma 2.28. There exists a scheme of finite type over \(k\), \(T_1\), \(t_1\in T_1(k)\) and \(f:X_1\longrightarrow T_1\), proper and flat, with fiber \(C\) at \(t_1\) and such that for every \(u\in T_1(k)\), the completion of \(T_1\) at \(u\) is a formal moduli scheme for the fiber \(X_{1u}\).

Here is a direct proof of this corollary of general results of Artin. Let \(\mathcal L\) be a very ample invertible sheaf on \(C\), such that \(H^1(C,\mathcal L)=0\), and \(\mathbb P=\mathbb P(H^0(C,\mathcal L))\). We have \(v:C\hookrightarrow\mathbb P\). Let \(T_2\) be the Hilbert scheme, classifying the subvarieties of \(\mathbb P\) with the same Hilbert polynomial as \(v(C)\subset\mathbb P\). Let \(t_2\in T_2\) correspond to \(v(C)\). The completion \(\widehat T_2\) of \(T_2\) at \(t_2\) is smooth over \(S_0\), the vertical tangent bundle of \(\widehat T_2/T_0\) being deduced from the subbundle \(L\) of the tangent bundle of \(T_2\) which is the image of \(\operatorname{Lie}(\operatorname{Aut}(\mathbb P))\) for the action of \(\operatorname{Aut}(\mathbb P)\) on \(T_2\). In a neighborhood of \(t_2\), let \(f_1,\ldots,f_n\), \(n=\dim\operatorname{Aut}(\mathbb P)\), be equations defining a subscheme \(T'_1\) of \(T_2\) passing through \(t_2\) and whose completion at \(t_2\) is étale over \(T_0\). We take for \(T_1\) a neighborhood of \(t_2\) in \(T'_1\) where the tangent bundle is transverse to \(L\).

2.29. To the morphism of functors \[(\text{deformations of }C)\longrightarrow(\text{deformations of }A_s)\] corresponds a morphism \(p:T_0\longrightarrow T\). By VI 2.10, \(p\) is smooth. Let \(t\) be the closed point of \(T\). The Zariski tangent space of \(p^{-1}(t)\) at \(t_0\) classifies the locally trivial deformations of \(C\) over \(k[\varepsilon]\) (\(\varepsilon^2=0\)). Setting \(E_0=p^{-1}(E)\), we thus have
\[(2.29.1)\qquad \dim(E_0)=\dim(E)+\dim H^1(C,\mathcal T^1_{C/k}).\]

Since \(X_0/T_0\) is flat, the fibers of \(X_0/T_0\) all have the same arithmetic genus \(p\) (\(1-p=\chi(\mathcal O)\)). By hypothesis, the fiber at the generic point of \(E_0\) is smooth; the formal moduli scheme of a proper smooth curve being smooth of dimension \(3p-3\), we deduce from 2.28 that
Let \(C^{\sim}\) be the normalization of \(C\), of arithmetic genus \(p^{\sim}=p-\delta(s)\). We have
\[(2.29.3)\qquad \dim H^1(C,\mathcal T^1_{C/k})=-\chi(C,\mathcal T^1_{C/k}) =-\chi(C^{\sim},\mathcal T^1_{C^{\sim}/k})+m_1(s) =-[3(p-\delta(s))-3]+m_1(s).\]

blocks.title: Conjecture 2.30.
blocks.html: Conjecture 2.30. Every reduced proper curve has a smooth deformation.
This conjecture implies, by 2.27 and 2.28, that the formal versal scheme of deformations of \(A_s\) is always equidimensional, of dimension \(3\delta(s)-m_1(s)\).
2.31. Let \(C\) be a reduced proper curve over \(k\), of arithmetic genus \(p\). We will denote by

\(M_0\): the dimension of the formal versal scheme \(T\) of deformations of \(C\);
\(M_1\): the dimension of the Lie algebra of \(\operatorname{Aut}(C)\).
For \(E\) an irreducible component of \(T\), we will write \(M_0^E\) for the dimension of \(E\).
The global analogue of 2.27 is the following.

blocks.title: Proposition 2.32.
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.32. If the deformation of \(C\) over the generic point of \(E\) is smooth, we have
\[3p-3=M_0^E-M_1.\]

Let \(X\subset C(k)\) be a finite set of smooth points of \(C\), such that every vector field on \(C\) vanishing at every point \(x\in X\) is zero. The deformation functor of \((C,X)\) is prorepresentable, and satisfies a variant of 2.28. The formal moduli scheme of \((C,X)\) is smooth over \(T\), of relative dimension \(\#X-M_1\), and from the analogue of 2.28 we deduce that
whence 2.32.
Suppose \(k\) of characteristic 0. We then have \(M_1=\dim\operatorname{Aut}(C)\). Here is an analogous interpretation of \(m_1(s)\).

blocks.title: Lemma 2.33.
blocks.html: Lemma 2.33. If \(k\) is of characteristic 0, every derivation of \(A_s\) extends to a derivation of \(\widetilde A_s\).

If \(D\) is a derivation of \(A_s\), \(\exp(Dt)=\sum D^n t^n/n!\) is an automorphism of the completion at \((s,0)\) of \(A_s\otimes_k k[t]\). By transport of structure, this automorphism acts on the normalization \((\widetilde A_s\otimes_k k[t])^\wedge\) of this ring. Reducing mod \(t^2\), we find an automorphism \(1+Dt\) of \(\widetilde A_s{}^\wedge\otimes_k k[t]/(t^2)\), and \(D\) is thus extended to a derivation of \(\widetilde A_s{}^\wedge\). Since it also extends to the field of fractions of \(A_s\) (total ring of fractions if \(A_s\) is not integral), it extends to \(\widetilde A_s\).
Suppose \(s\) singular. An analogous argument then shows that the vector field on \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widetilde A_s)\) defined by \(D\) vanishes at every point above \(s\).

Let \(c\) be an ideal of \(\widetilde A_s\) contained in the conductor of \(\widetilde A_s/A_s\). Let \(\widetilde G_c=\operatorname{Aut}(\widetilde A_s/c)\) and let \(G_c\) be the subgroup of \(\widetilde G_c\) stabilizing the subalgebra \(A_s/c\). For \(d\subset c\), \(\widetilde G_d\) stabilizes \(c/d\) and maps onto \(\widetilde G_c\) (note that \(g\in\widetilde G_c\) is determined by \(g(t)\), for \(t\) a uniformizer, and that \(g(t)\) is any uniformizer). The pointed homogeneous space \(\widetilde G_c/G_c\) is thus independent of \(c\). We write \(\widetilde G/G\).
Let \(\widetilde\tau_0\) be the \(\widetilde A_s\)-module of vector fields on \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widetilde A_s)\) vanishing at the points above \(s\). For \(s\) singular, we have
\[ \dim_k(\widetilde\tau/\widetilde\tau_0)=\text{number of branches}\ ; \] \[ \widetilde\tau_0/\tau\simeq\text{tangent space at the origin of }\widetilde G/G. \]

blocks.title: Proposition 2.34.
blocks.html: Proposition 2.34. In characteristic 0 and for \(s\) singular, the integer \(m_1(s)\) is the sum of the number of branches and the dimension of \(\widetilde G/G\).
